abstract:
This study investigates the impact of various technological devices and connectivity on the academic achievement of high school seniors in Denizli, Turkey. Utilizing a survey research design, data were collected from 533 students across 27 different schools. Academic success was measured using scores from the "Access to Higher Education" (AHE) exam, a critical component of the national university entrance system. Through the application of parametric statistical analyses, including independent samples t-tests and one-way ANOVA, the research evaluated the influence of personal cell phone ownership, mobile internet access, home computer availability, home internet connectivity, and the density of television sets in the household.

The empirical findings reveal that students who possess a home computer and those with a dedicated home internet connection achieved significantly higher exam scores compared to their peers without such resources. Conversely, no statistically significant relationship was found between academic performance and the ownership of cell phones, the availability of mobile internet, or the number of televisions in the home. These results suggest that while mobile devices may serve diverse social or recreational functions, fixed computing resources remain vital tools for fostering self-learning and cognitive development. The study concludes that the positive educational contributions of e-learning tools outweigh potential distractions, supporting the integration of computer-assisted instruction to enhance student outcomes.
